questions.q: What type of tone is used in a persuasive article?
questions.explanation: Persuasive articles use emotional tone to convince readers.

questions.q: Which of the following is not a characteristic of a good article?
questions.explanation: A good article should be concise and to the point, not lengthy.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of an article?
questions.explanation: Articles aim to provide information on a specific topic.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a persuasive device?
questions.explanation: Rhetorical questions are used to engage readers and persuade them.

questions.q: What is the difference between a descriptive and a narrative article?
opts: (A) Descriptive articles are longer than narrative articles.
(B) Narrative articles focus on events, while descriptive articles focus on people.
(C) Descriptive articles are more formal than narrative articles.
(D) Narrative articles are more concise than descriptive articles.
questions.explanation: Narrative articles focus on events, while descriptive articles focus on people, places, or objects.

questions.q: Which of the following is not a characteristic of a well-structured article?
opts: (D) A mix of paragraphs and poetry
questions.explanation: Articles should be written in prose, not poetry.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a thesis statement in an article?
questions.explanation: The thesis statement clearly states the main argument or claim of the article.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a rhetorical device used in an article?
questions.explanation: Juxtaposition is used to contrast ideas and engage the reader.

questions.q: What is the difference between a persuasive and a descriptive article?
opts: (A) Persuasive articles aim to entertain the reader, while descriptive articles aim to inform.
(B) Descriptive articles focus on events, while persuasive articles focus on people.
(C) Persuasive articles aim to inform the reader, while descriptive articles aim to persuade.
(D) Descriptive articles are more persuasive than persuasive articles.
questions.explanation: Persuasive articles aim to inform the reader and persuade them to a particular point of view, while descriptive articles aim to describe people, places, or objects.

questions.q: Which of the following is not a characteristic of a good article title?
questions.explanation: A good article title should be concise and to the point, not lengthy.
questions.topic: Characteristics of a Good Article Title

questions.q: What is the purpose of a conclusion in an article?
questions.explanation: The conclusion reiterates the main argument and leaves a lasting impression on the reader.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a fallacy in an article?
questions.explanation: All of the above are examples of fallacies used in articles.

questions.q: What is the difference between a logical fallacy and a rhetorical device?
opts: (A) Logical fallacies are used to persuade the reader, while rhetorical devices are used to engage the reader.
(B) Rhetorical devices are used to persuade the reader, while logical fallacies are used to engage the reader.
(C) Logical fallacies are errors in reasoning, while rhetorical devices are used to enhance the writing.
(D) Rhetorical devices are used to enhance the writing, while logical fallacies are errors in reasoning.
questions.explanation: Rhetorical devices are used to enhance the writing, while logical fallacies are errors in reasoning.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a logical fallacy?
questions.explanation: All of the above are examples of logical fallacies.

questions.q: What is the purpose of an introduction in an article?
questions.explanation: The introduction introduces the topic and sets the tone for the article.

questions.q: Which of the following is not a characteristic of a well-written article?
questions.explanation: A well-written article should be well-edited and free of errors.

questions.q: What is the difference between a persuasive article and a narrative article?
opts: (A) Persuasive articles focus on events, while narrative articles focus on people.
(B) Narrative articles aim to entertain the reader, while persuasive articles aim to inform.
(C) Persuasive articles focus on people, while narrative articles focus on events.
(D) Narrative articles aim to persuade the reader, while persuasive articles aim to narrate.
questions.explanation: Persuasive articles focus on people, while narrative articles focus on events.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a persuasive article?
opts: (A) A descriptive article about a person
(B) A narrative article about a historical event
(C) An article that argues for a particular point of view
(D) A how-to article about a skill
questions.explanation: The article argues for a particular point of view, making it a persuasive article.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a transitional phrase in an article?
questions.explanation: Transitional phrases are used to connect ideas and create a logical flow in the article.
